reviews.author: PARTHA BHATTACHARJEE
reviews.text: An old wooden bridge built by the Japanese traders in late 17/18th century, at ancient city HoiAn. A marvellous piece of artwork with a temple in the bridge. The atmosphere in night is worth watching with lights glittering everywhere and looks like everyone is in a celebration mood. A very vibrant surroundings and enjoyed every moment there. Highly recommended.

reviews.author: Moonlight85
reviews.text: Japanese Covered Bridge (also known as the Lai Vien Bridge or Cau Pagoda) located in the heart of Hoi An Ancient Town, Vietnam. It is considered a symbol of Hoi An and is a nationally recognized landmark. The bridge was built in the 1590s by Japanese merchants to connect the Japanese quarter with the Chinese quarter across a narrow canal. The architecture is a unique blend of Japanese, Chinese, and Vietnamese styles, featuring a stunning wooden pagoda and detailed carvings. Hoi An Ancient Town is a UNESCO World Heritage Site, and the bridge is a major historical and cultural attraction for visitors.

reviews.author: Emma Thomas
reviews.text: Very busy and difficult to get photographs as people needing to walk by (not their fault, just going places as are we all). Closer to the centre than I thought and walked past it twice without realising (that’s just me, so much to look at) all I’ll say is thank you for the clean up button on my phone 😃. Very beautiful bridge and the detail is amazing. Worth a look.

reviews.author: Harry Lim
reviews.text: Must visit early morning to see the Japanese Covered Bridge without the crowds! ​Hoi An Old Town is actually more charming in the morning than at night. Visiting early, I found the streets incredibly quiet and peaceful—it was hard to believe it was the same place that gets so crowded in the evening. ​Thanks to the early start, I could take photos of the Japanese Covered Bridge and the surrounding signature yellow buildings without any obstructions. Just strolling through the quiet streets before the shops opened was healing in itself. If you want to escape the tourist crowds and feel the authentic, serene atmosphere of Hoi An, I highly recommend an early morning walk.

reviews.author: Hemanth Shriyan
reviews.text: Walking around this beautiful bridge was a memorable experience. We explored cafes in this area and did last moment shopping . The place has a nice vibe and we loved it. Few vendors sold pop up greetings on the road and we did purchase many. Bridge itself provides a nice background for your pics.

reviews.author: Oscar Berto
reviews.text: "A beautiful and iconic symbol of Hoi An! This historical Japanese bridge is a must-see. The architecture is stunning, and it’s very well-preserved. It can get a bit crowded during the day, so I recommend visiting early in the morning or in the evening when it’s beautifully lit up. A great spot for photos and to learn about the town's history!"

reviews.author: John Gledhill
reviews.text: The 17th century Japanese covered bridge in Hoi An is an absolute must to see when in town. A wonderful piece of history set in a beautiful part of Hoi An. As you would expect, this bridge is very popular and gets rather busy but don’t let this stop you going. It’s a great memory making moment. Don’t miss out…. Go and visit.

reviews.author: YT Koh
reviews.text: An interesting bridge built with good solid wood. Was there before 6 or 7 years ago and we had to pay but now, this is not necessary. That helps as it brings the crowds from one side to the other. There is a Japanese museum one the side after crossing the bridge, they want tickets for that. A must go place when in Da Nang no matter how many times one is there, it is also imprinted on the back of the 20,000 Dong note!

reviews.author: noel medina
reviews.text: The Japanese Covered Bridge in Hội An, Quảng Nam Province, Vietnam is one of the most recognizable historic structures in Hội An’s Ancient Town and a key symbol of its cultural heritage. It serves as a pedestrian crossing, so patience is needed as it is often crowded with visitors throughout the day. For photos, it can be easier to shoot from nearby bridge, or wait until dusk when the lanterns are lit and the bridge feels more atmospheric and iconic. It is best treated as a short stop while exploring the Ancient Town rather than a place to linger.

reviews.author: Thuha Ng
reviews.text: Known as the Japanese Bridge or Lai Vien Bridge, it was built by Japanese merchants around the 17th century. The pagoda was considered a sword that struck down the monster Namazu, preventing it from thrashing and causing earthquakes. Later, a pagoda was added, connecting to the northern railing and protruding into the middle of the bridge, hence the name Chua Cau (or Pagoda Bridge). In 1719, Lord Nguyen Phuc Chu visited Hoi An and named the bridge Lai Vien Bridge, meaning "Bridge welcoming guests from afar." According to the dates recorded on the roof beams and the remaining stele at the bridge's head, the bridge was rebuilt in 1817. The pagoda was likely rebuilt around this time as well. The pagoda was restored in 1817, 1865, 1915, and 1986, gradually losing its Japanese architectural elements and instead adopting a style heavily influenced by Vietnamese and Chinese architecture.

reviews.author: Ines 063
reviews.text: The Chùa Cầu, or Japanese Covered Bridge, is a highly recommended and iconic symbol of Hoi An, famously featured on the 20,000 VND banknote. To experience its intricate interior architecture and the small pagoda inside, you will need an Old Town ticket, a great value for exploring this UNESCO World Heritage site. You can freely walk across the bridge and admire its exterior architecture without a ticket. However, to enter the inner shrine located at the center of the bridge, a valid Hoi An Ancient Town ticket is required. The ticket costs 120,000 VND and grants access to a total of five heritage attractions within the Old Town. These tickets can be purchased at official yellow booths located at major entrances to the Ancient Town. To avoid crowds and get the best photos without tourists in them, it is highly recommended to visit early in the morning, ideally before 8 am. The light is better, the weather is cooler, and you can enjoy the peaceful atmosphere before the main tourist influx begins. Admire the unique blend of Japanese, Chinese, and Vietnamese architectural styles, with a gracefully curved roof and intricate wooden carvings. Look for the statues of a pair of dogs and a pair of monkeys guarding the ends of the bridge, believed to symbolize the years of construction or important emperors. Your ticket also provides access to other notable sites nearby such as the Tan Ky Old House, the Fujian Assembly Hall, or local museums. The Japanese Bridge truly is a must-see landmark that encapsulates Hoi An's rich history and cultural fusion. Make sure to keep your ticket handy throughout your stay

reviews.author: Aidan & Marybeth
reviews.text: Free to walk across the bridge and take as many photos as you would like. If you buy a ticket from the ticket office nearby (170,000) - you can enter here and numerous other historical sites in the town. However, there is not a lot to see inside just an information board about the bridge - a quick Google could save you the time. Took around 5-10 minutes for everything, and most tours will bring you here 🙂

reviews.author: Manny Yu
reviews.text: 12/24/25. Footbridge with a Temple attached!⛩️ The bridge's temple has ornate decor, including porcelain bowls used to cap the ends of the roof tiles and statues of a monkey and a dog (the monkey god and the dog god are the two gods who control Namazu in Japanese folklore. Chữ Hán can be found throughout the bridge and temple, including "Lai Viễn Kiều" in front of the entrance of the temple. In 1593, Japanese merchants migrated to Hoi Ano and began building a covered bridge to cross a neighborhood canal. They finished construction in 1595 and improved access between the Chinese enclave on the other side. In 1653, Japanese residents built a temple atop the bridge as a way to, according to legend, placate the earthquake-inducing monster Namazu. Must visit Hoi An stop when doing a day tour from Da nang.

reviews.author: Francine Laura
reviews.text: Chùa Cầu, the Japanese covered bridge, was built in 1595 by Japanese merchants, connecting Hoi An's old Japanese and Chinese quarters. It is a key landmark in the city and the picture is featured on the 20,000 VND bill. There is a small shrine in the bridge and monkeys guard the entrance. The bridge went through a major renovation in 2024.

reviews.author: Gaurav Agrawal
reviews.text: A small but very beautiful bridge made by Japanese traders to connect two alleys in old part of hoi an city One of crowded place in Vietnam You need a ticket to enter/pass through bridge , A combine ticket which includes 5 site visit in hoi an is valid here and can be purchased at the entrance of old city of hoi an This bridge also has a old temple dedicated to Japanese diety This bridge is very famous among people you want good photographs Visit early in day to avoid crowds

reviews.author: Peter Laytham
reviews.text: The rich history of Hoi An is captured beautifully by this landmark bridge and the nearby Chinese temple, but the true charm of old Hoi An lies in the glorious street scene with shops, restaurants and coffee outlets galore, and as far as we could tell they’re all awesome. Each evening the locals put on events like the lantern parade on the river, and the spectacular Hoi An memories show which I personally cannot recommend highly enough.

reviews.author: Dimitri Sterlin
reviews.text: It's a nice bridge but don't get your expectations too high! You'll enjoy taking pictures there if it's not too crowded. I think this place is more interesting if you are into historical stuff, the origin of the bridge, architecture, etc. Other that that... It's a bridge!

reviews.author: Thomas
reviews.text: A historic bridge, well maintained with a small temple/shrine in the middle. Only thing we didn’t fully understand is it seems to be free for everyone to visit, but we paid for the historic city pass which included this bridge visit. I proactively asked the security there and only then he scanned my pass, all a little confusing. I’d say try to visit first before you buy the pass (of course the pass covers 5 sites in total and not just the Japanese bridge) and then determine whether you need to pay for it or not.

reviews.author: Russ Lacuata
reviews.text: The Hoi An Covered Bridge is known a the tourist symbol of the Old Town. One of the highlights on Hoi An tourism with its quiet street scenery. The pagoda is designed with wood imbued with Japanese style with a graceful roof and the foundation is made of stone pillars.

reviews.author: Manoj Telrandhe
reviews.text: The best place to visit in Vietnam. Best time to visit is evening and night. Colourful shops, decorated with colorful lantern, river, bridge, boating. Awesome atmosphere.

reviews.author: 古川
reviews.text: When I first visited Hoi An years ago, the bridge was free and quiet with few tourists. I sat nearby, watching seawater flood the streets as locals rode bikes through it—like walking into a flowing painting. Known by its elegant Chinese name "Laiyuanqiao", it was built by Japanese merchants between 1593 and 1595, linking the Japanese and Chinese quarters as a bond between two civilizations. With a delicate Japanese covered bridge design, stone monkeys and dogs at its ends mark its construction years and wishes for peace. In 1719, the Nguyen Dynasty named it "welcome guests from afar", and its Chinese calligraphy blends with the wooden structure. After the Edo period, the Chinese maintained it, and the central Buddhist shrine’s incense has burned for 400 years, witnessing cultural integration. Today, it’s Hoi An’s totem; sitting by it, I realized travel is stumbling upon distant ingenuity in historic relics.

reviews.author: Gmp-lucky anch
reviews.text: Great view when going across the bridge at night because the little boats with lanterns on them light up.🚨ALERT FOR PRAMS there is a side ways wood plank attached so you have to lift it over.ALERT FOR PRAMS🚨

reviews.author: GOONER.
reviews.text: You can’t visit Hoi An without seeing the Japanese Covered Bridge. It’s the town’s ultimate symbol, though it’s always packed with tourists jostling for photos. After the recent renovation, it looks much newer, which is a bit polarizing. Still, the historical vibe and its unique architecture are unmissable. Just grab a quick photo and enjoy the ancient street view!🖤🧡❤

reviews.author: Nhất Trần
reviews.text: The temple's architecture dates back to the 16th century, hundreds of years ago, when this place was a bustling port, a blend of Japanese, Chinese, Taiwanese, British, French, and Vietnamese cultures. Everything is free, but if you want to explore further, you can buy a ticket to go inside. It's a worthwhile place to visit when in Hoi An Ancient Town. My hometown is 167km away, but this is my first time here. Rất đáng để trải nghiệm!

reviews.author: Expeditionsolo Luke
reviews.text: Beautiful ancient bridge! UNESCO world heritage site. Very busy but great for pictures. Lots around the area to see and explore.

reviews.author: Chloe Cocker
reviews.text: Interesting stopping place in Hoi An. On the tourist ticket list but no one asked to see the ticket so could have got it free. About 10 minutes needed but the nearby souvenir shops and other old buildings are worth a visit.

reviews.author: Jolyn Fang
reviews.text: Pretty. Loads of people. Just reopened last night since the recent flooding. The bridge is quite well-maintained.. makes a pretty backdrop for the lantern boats on the river.

reviews.author: Mcw Chow
reviews.text: If you’re visiting Hoi An, don’t miss this little gem! The whole town feels full of history and charm, and this shop fits right in. Khoi Khoi Design has so many beautiful handmade dresses — unique pieces for both adults and kids. The quality and design really stand out, and each piece feels one of a kind. The owner was so kind and patient, helping me find the perfect fit. I love that everything is locally made — such a meaningful souvenir to bring home. Definitely worth a visit if you have 2 days in Hoi An!

reviews.author: Chiang Paul
reviews.text: The Japanese Bridge is a must-see in Huian Ancient Town. Today is February 15th, and tomorrow is Lunar New Year's Eve. Many tourists are visiting here.

reviews.author: Manish _
reviews.text: as part of Monkey Mountain – Marble Mountains – Coconut Jungle – Hoi An City Full-day tour, our last leg was in Hoi An old town. Our tour guide walked us through the Hoi An old town bylanes starting with silk shop which showed as silk worms to silk thread extraction to weaving, was pretty expensive, given teh intricate work done. Then we walked through the Japanese quarter, chinese quarter, shopped around (things are more expensive than in DaNang, but still got good options with some bargains - still the same dress that we bought here (after bargain) was quoted 30% less in Danang night market (son tra). There were lot of beautiful lanterns with microfibre and bamboo/wood inside, we bought one as these are extremely beautiful. The Japanese bridge was very old and gives a very good peak at the history of Hoi An. we even visited a 100+ year old house (mostly converted to selling specific items/museum), but gave a peak of history and was maintained very well. WE went for a wonderful dinner on a restaurant on this road and then headed out for a boat tour on Hoai River and released the flower lanterns to pray for good luck.
